metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct S3's (94f9) file_impact to list src/rebar/_config_schema.py (add) and remove src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py and src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py.
evidence: S3 file_impact: {path: 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py', reason: 'Add reconciler.backend key, default jira'}, {path: 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py', reason: 'Static-frozenset validation of backend key'}
S3 description: 'No src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py change and no new rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py'
S3 description: 'the real config home is the app-level src/rebar/_config_schema.py above'
S3 AC: 'ReconcilerConfig.backend exists in src/rebar/_config_schema.py' — the AC correctly names the right file, but the file_impact metadata does not.
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: S3's (94f9) file_impact metadata lists 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py' and 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py' as edit targets, directly contradicting the story description body which states 'No src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py change and no new rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py — the real config home is the app-level src/rebar/_config_schema.py.' An implementer following the file_impact would edit the wrong files.
scenarios: An implementer uses file_impact as the authoritative edit list and edits src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py (flat domain constants) instead of src/rebar/_config_schema.py, introducing changes to the wrong config module and potentially breaking the domain-constants module.
A reviewer's diff-checker flags unexpected files not in file_impact (src/rebar/_config_schema.py) while finding no changes to the listed files, creating confusion.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the two wrong file_impact entries with: {path: 'src/rebar/_config_schema.py', reason: 'Add backend field to ReconcilerConfig + _as_choice coercer in reconciler coercer table'}.

findings.finding: Parent AC4 states 'all ~29 test patch strings are migrated with no re-export shims' as a measurable deliverable, but child dfb9 explicitly states the '~29 mock.patch strings' count is wrong — there are zero mock.patch string patches — and reclassifies the test surface into three categories with different counts (~23 object-level patches unaffected, ~18 path-constant files, ~18 dotted-import files). The parent metric cannot be satisfied as written because the child contradicts its own unit of measure.
findings.location: child dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9, Acceptance Criteria AC4 of parent epic, and dfb9 Testing/Scope sections
findings.suggested_fix: Update parent AC4 to read: 'the category-2 (~18 path-constant) and category-3 (~18 dotted-import) test targets are rewritten to adapters.jira.* paths atomically with no re-export shims — verified by grep returning zero hits for old root paths (excluding path-agnostic patch.object sites).'

findings.finding: Parent AC-4 states 'all ~29 test patch strings are migrated with no re-export shims (S5)' but S5 (dfb9) explicitly contradicts this, saying 'the first draft's ~29 mock.patch strings was wrong; there are zero mock.patch string patches' — replacing the concept with categories 2 (~18 path-constant files) and 3 (~18 dotted-import files). The parent AC uses erroneous terminology that S5 does not reconcile upward.
findings.location: child dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9 — Context/Problem ('characterized correctly — the first draft's ~29 mock.patch strings was wrong'); parent Acceptance Criteria AC-4
findings.suggested_fix: In parent AC-4, replace 'all ~29 test patch strings are migrated with no re-export shims' with 'the category-2 (~18 path-constant) and category-3 (~18 dotted-import) test references are rewritten to adapters.jira.* paths (zero mock.patch string patches exist; the ~23 patch.object _load_acli tests are path-agnostic and verified still-green), with no re-export shims — per S5's corrected taxonomy.'

findings.finding: S3's file_impact list includes 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py' (reason: 'Add reconciler.backend key, default jira') and 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py' (reason: 'Static-frozenset validation of backend key') — but the S3 description body explicitly states: 'No src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py change and no new rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py — the earlier draft named both in error.' The contradictory file_impact entries will cause an implementer following the file list to open and edit the wrong files.
findings.location: S3 (94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75) file_impact list vs. description body
findings.suggested_fix: Replace S3 file_impact entries: remove `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py` and `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py`; add `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` with reason 'Add backend field to ReconcilerConfig + _as_choice coercer in reconciler coercer table'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the capability-detection mechanism (e.g., `isinstance(backend, SupportsLinks)`, a `Backend.capabilities() -> frozenset[type]` method, or a `backend.supports(SupportsLinks)` predicate) in ADR 0035 §(d) and co-locate the decision rule with the capability Protocol definitions in `_backend.py`'s planned spec.
evidence: S2 Testing: 'a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it (observed via behavior — a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links), not via structural introspection'.
The Design section names the three capability Protocols but gives no dispatch rule: 'opt-in capability Protocols' with no further specification.

No story (S2, S3, S4, or S5) specifies the mechanism by which the core checks backend capability: isinstance(), hasattr(), a backend.capabilities() method, a Backend.supports() predicate, or a registry annotation.
findings.finding: The three opt-in capability Protocols (`SupportsLinks`, `SupportsComments`, `SupportsIncremental`) are referenced in the plan with the rule that a backend is 'never asked to sync links' unless it advertises `SupportsLinks`, and detection is 'not via structural introspection' — but the positive detection mechanism is never specified anywhere in the plan. The contract suite AC (S2) says 'a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it (observed via behavior)' but gives no dispatch rule. This is a decision-rule gap: the processing protocol for capability detection is not co-located with the capability Protocol schema.
findings.location: S2 Testing section (ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798) / Design section of epic plan
scenarios: S4 implementer routes links through the Backend port but must invent the capability-check mechanism (isinstance vs. hasattr vs. Backend.supports()), producing code that the contract suite cannot catch as wrong because the suite only checks 'behavior' without specifying what behavior to assert.

A second backend (epic be74) implements SupportsLinks but not SupportsComments; if the detection mechanism is ad-hoc (e.g., hasattr), a method-name collision in the new backend silently enables a capability it shouldn't.

findings.finding: Parent AC-7 states 'adding a backend requires only a new adapters/<x>/ implementing the pinned interface — verified by the proof-of-seam FakeBackend passing the contract suite with no core edit,' but S3 (94f9) design requires editing _config_schema.py (a core app-level config file, not adapters/<x>/) to add the new key to the _as_choice static set for every new backend.
findings.location: child 94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75 — Approach ('A second backend adds its key to both the _as_choice set and the registry'); parent Acceptance Criteria AC-7
findings.suggested_fix: Option A (update AC-7): Revise parent AC-7 to read 'adding a backend requires implementing adapters/<x>/ and adding its key to the _as_choice set in _config_schema.py and the registry.' Option B (redesign S3): derive the _as_choice allowed-set dynamically from the populated registry at config-load time rather than hardcoding it, so only the adapters/<x>/ addition is needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a method-signature table for each of the five role Protocols (`TicketTransport`, `OutboundMapper`, `InboundMapper`, `FieldSanitizer`, `IdentityConvention`) to ADR 0035 §(d), and update S1's AC to verify that table is present — so the pinned interface is a greppable spec, not 'derive from current code.'
evidence: ADR §(d) currently reads: 'To add backend X, create adapters/<x>/ providing the operations in (b): a transport/CRUD module ... outbound field mapping ... inbound field extraction ... sanitization/limits ... identity convention.' — prose bullets only, no method signatures.

S1 AC: '§(d) names all five role Protocols, the Backend facade, RemoteRef, and the registry keyed on config.reconciler.backend' — verified by reading the rendered section. No AC line requires method signatures to appear.
S2 Approach: 'Derive each Protocol's method signatures from the de-facto surface the core already calls' — the pinned interface is derived at implementation time by grep, not from the ADR spec.
S2 AC: 'importable via from rebar._engine.rebar_reconciler._backend import Backend, RemoteRef and RemoteRef(...) constructs' — verifies names and constructibility only, not signature completeness.
findings.finding: The five role Protocol method signatures are referenced throughout the plan (S2 implements them, S4 callers route through them, the contract suite verifies them) but are never enumerated anywhere in the plan or in the current ADR §(d). S1's AC only verifies that §(d) names the Protocol *names* and `RemoteRef` — not that it pins the actual method signatures. An S2 implementer is directed to 'derive each Protocol's method signatures from the de-facto surface the core already calls, so JiraBackend is a pure delegation shim,' meaning the authoritative spec is 'grep the existing code,' not a pinned interface table. This is a processing-protocol/decision-rule gap: the rules (method signatures) are not co-located with the schema (Protocol names) that S2 and the contract suite depend on.
findings.location: S1 AC (ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb) / ADR 0035 §(d) / S2 Approach section (ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798)
scenarios: An S2 implementer produces a TicketTransport.update() signature that differs from what S4 callers expect, because the 'grep the code' derivation step finds a different overload than intended — with no pinned spec to arbitrate.
The contract suite (ce4d) verifies round-trips but cannot catch a signature mismatch if both JiraBackend and FakeBackend are derived from the same grep baseline rather than from a pinned spec.
findings.suggested_fix: In the S1 ADR §(d) rewrite, include a code-block or table listing each Protocol's method names, parameter types, and return types (even if simplified). Update S1 AC from 'names all five role Protocols' to 'names all five role Protocols and their method signatures (verified by reading the table in §(d))'.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In S4, pin the evolved `_load_acli` seam's return type (e.g., `TicketTransport` or `Backend`) and specify what the updated test doubles must inject (e.g., `return_value=mock_transport` rather than `SimpleNamespace(AcliClient=...)`) so the ~50 `patch.object` patch sites have an unambiguous migration target.
evidence: The current `_load_acli()` returns `acli` module; callers do `acli_mod.AcliClient(jira_url=..., user=..., api_token=...)` (applier.py:439, :594; fetcher.py:367).

~50 test files inject `SimpleNamespace(AcliClient=lambda **_: mock_client)` via `patch.object(applier|fetcher, '_load_acli', return_value=...)` (confirmed by grep: test_applier.py, test_fetcher_snapshot.py, test_reconcile_once.py, test_reconcile_idempotency.py, etc.).

S4 states 'the seam keeps returning something whose transport those doubles satisfy' but does not specify what the evolved seam returns (a Backend? a TicketTransport? still a module-like namespace?) or how the ~50 test doubles need to change — whether they become `SimpleNamespace(transport=mock_transport)` or some other shape.
If callers post-S4 call `backend.transport` (a TicketTransport) but the existing test doubles still inject `.AcliClient`, every test that patches `_load_acli` would AttributeError at the call site.
findings.finding: S4's evolved `_load_acli` seam return-type contract is unspecified, leaving the mechanism for keeping ~50 existing `patch.object(applier|fetcher, '_load_acli', ...)` test doubles working after the seam changes ambiguous.
findings.location: S4 description: '`applier`/`fetcher` — evolve their existing `_load_acli` seams to yield the backend transport and update their `patch.object(..., "_load_acli")` tests in the same change'
scenarios: An implementer evolves `_load_acli` to return a `Backend` and updates the ~2 production call sites in applier.py and fetcher.py, but does not update the 50 test doubles that still inject SimpleNamespace(AcliClient=...) — all those tests now fail with AttributeError at the `.AcliClient(...)` call in the evolved seam wrapper.
An implementer makes the seam return a TicketTransport directly, but the existing tests inject a module-shaped namespace — mismatch in the protocol expected at the call site.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to S4's approach: 'After S4, `_load_acli()` in `applier.py` and `fetcher.py` returns a `TicketTransport` (the `.transport` of the selected `Backend`). The ~N `patch.object(applier|fetcher, "_load_acli", return_value=...)` test doubles are updated to inject a `mock_transport` satisfying the `TicketTransport` Protocol (e.g., `MagicMock(spec=TicketTransport)`) rather than a module namespace with `.AcliClient`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC line to S4 (6673) asserting outbound_comments.py has no remaining lazy_load of adf.py or comment_limits.py after the Backend routing, and add outbound_comments.py to S5's (dfb9) AC enumeration (or confirm S4's AC makes S5 coverage moot).
evidence: Parent AC: 'the two non-moved root modules that reference them by sibling filename (inbound_fields.py, outbound_comments.py) are repointed'
S5 scope: 'Any outbound_comments.py / inbound_translate.py / reconcile_check.py adf/comment_limits loader refs not already eliminated by S4 — grep-confirmed at S5 time; repoint or drop.'
S5 AC enumerates: jira_fields.py:18, inbound_fields.py:36, acli_cli_ops.py:36 — outbound_comments.py is absent from the AC list.
S4 scope: routes outbound_comments.py:84,100 lazy_load of adf.py + comment_limits.py through Backend. But S4 AC does not assert 'outbound_comments.py has no remaining sibling-filename loader refs'.
If S4 partially routes (e.g., one of the two lazy_load calls is missed), no AC in either story catches the survivor.
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: The parent AC for PAC-4 explicitly names outbound_comments.py as one of 'the two non-moved root modules that reference them by sibling filename' that must be 'repointed' in S5, but S5's AC never enumerates outbound_comments.py and defers its coverage to a conditional 'grep-confirmed at S5 time' check. S4 routes outbound_comments.py's lazy_load calls through the Backend port (eliminating rather than repointing them), but S4's AC does not explicitly confirm outbound_comments.py's sibling-filename loader references are fully eliminated, leaving an owned-by-none gap if either story handles it incompletely.
findings.location: child dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9 (Scope and AC sections); child 6673-7636-a116-4f90 (Scope section)
scenarios: S4 routes outbound_comments.py:100 but misses :84; S5's grep-at-time check finds it and has no AC to enforce fix; the gap is noticed only during implementation, not plan review.
Both S4 and S5 are reviewed as green but outbound_comments.py retains a root-path reference that breaks after S5's physical move.
findings.suggested_fix: In S4 AC, add: 'outbound_comments.py:84 and :100 lazy_load calls are eliminated (routed through Backend.sanitizer); verified by grep for lazy_load.*adf\|lazy_load.*comment_limits in outbound_comments.py returning zero hits.' In S5 AC, note explicitly that outbound_comments.py was handled in S4 (or enumerate it if any residual survives).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update S5's (dfb9) title to remove '~29 patch strings' and accurately reflect the actual migration: category-2 (~18 path-constant) and category-3 (~18 dotted-import) test-target rewrites.
evidence: S5 title: 'Relocate acli*/adf/outbound_fields/comment_limits into adapters/jira/ + teach loader; migrate ~29 patch strings atomically'
S5 body: 'the first draft's "~29 mock.patch strings" was wrong; there are zero mock.patch("rebar_reconciler.acli…") string patches'

S5 body describes three categories: ~23 object-level patch.object (unaffected), ~18 path-constant files (must rewrite), ~18 dotted-import files (must rewrite) — none of which are string patches of the form the title implies.
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: S5's (dfb9) title 'migrate ~29 patch strings' is a stale first-draft artifact that directly contradicts the story body, which explicitly states 'there are zero mock.patch("rebar_reconciler.acli…") string patches' and recharacterizes the actual migration as categories 2+3 (path constants + dotted imports). The ~29 string-patch number is specifically called out in the body as wrong.
findings.location: child dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9 (title vs. body/Test surface section)
scenarios: An implementer scopes their work to find/fix 29 string patches (mock.patch('rebar_reconciler.acli...')) and declares done when they find zero, without addressing the ~18 category-2 path constants and ~18 category-3 dotted imports.
A reviewer cross-checking title against AC scope concludes the title and body describe different migrations, raising unnecessary plan integrity questions during implementation.
findings.suggested_fix: Retitle S5 as: 'Relocate acli*/adf/outbound_fields/comment_limits into adapters/jira/ + teach loader; rewrite ~18 path-constant + ~18 dotted-import test targets atomically'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to S5's scope an explicit audit of docs/, README, ADR 0035, and inline comments for references to the old root-level paths of relocated modules (acli*, adf, outbound_fields, comment_limits), and update or remove stale references as part of the atomic S5 commit.
evidence: 'migrate the ~29 test patch strings atomically' — scope is limited to test patch strings
No mention of scanning docs/, README, or code comments for stale root-level module-path references
ADR 0035 itself may reference old locations if it was written before S5's relocation decision
findings.finding: After S5 relocates acli*/adf/outbound_fields/comment_limits from the package root to adapters/jira/, any existing documentation, architecture diagrams, or inline comments that reference the old root-level module paths will become stale. The plan migrates ~29 test patch strings but does not call out auditing non-test doc references for these renamed paths.
scenarios: A developer reads ADR 0035 or a README after S5 and sees references to `rebar_reconciler/adf.py` (old root path) that no longer exist, causing confusion about module locations.
An architecture diagram generated from module paths shows stale entries, misleading future contributors about where vendor code lives.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete form of the 'neutrality gate' (e.g., 'a `tests/architecture/test_core_neutrality.py` module using `importlib`/`ast` to assert no `adapters.jira` or root-vendor imports in the 12 named core files, run as part of `make test`') so the AC is unambiguously evaluable in-session.
evidence: 'enforced by a neutrality gate on the 12 rewired core sites (the 8 vendor-importing modules + the 4 transport/construction sites — matching S4's own AC count)'
The plan names the gate's predicate (no imports of adapters.jira.* or root vendor modules) but never states the gate's implementation form or where it lives (test file, CI step, lint config).
findings.finding: The 'neutrality gate on the 12 rewired core sites' referenced in the S4 AC is described only by effect ('the backend-neutral core no longer imports adapters.jira.* or the root vendor modules') without specifying its concrete form — e.g., a pytest import-check fixture, a CI lint rule, a custom script — leaving the executor to choose an appropriate mechanism and leaving the criterion's in-session evaluability implicit.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — S4 bullet: 'enforced by a neutrality gate on the 12 rewired core sites'
scenarios: An executor implementing S4 must invent the gate mechanism; two executors might choose incompatible forms (one writes a pytest test, another adds a flake8 plugin), making the AC non-portable across reviewers.
Without a specified gate, a reviewer cannot determine whether the AC is 'green' from CI output alone — they must locate and run the gate manually, undermining in-session evaluability.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the S4 AC: 'The gate is implemented as `tests/architecture/test_core_neutrality.py`, which uses AST import-scanning to assert that none of the 12 named core modules contain a direct import of `adapters.jira.*` or the relocated root-vendor modules, and is executed by `make test`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite the S4 child decomposition description to replace 'move ADF-sizing + lossy-status/parent + duplicated maps/link-constants into adapters/jira/' with language that reflects caller-only rewiring (e.g., 'route calls to ADF-sizing, lossy-status/parent, and link-constants through the Backend port, leaving the files at the package root').
evidence: S4 body: 'move ADF-sizing + lossy-status/parent + duplicated maps/link-constants into adapters/jira/'
AC: 'The vendor files still physically reside at the package root after S4; only their *callers* change here. Their physical relocation into `adapters/jira/` completes in S5'
Boundary note: 'S4 = who-calls-what … S5 = where-files-live'
findings.finding: S4's child decomposition text says 'move ADF-sizing + lossy-status/parent + duplicated maps/link-constants into `adapters/jira/`' — which implies physical file relocation — but the S4 AC and the boundary summary note both explicitly state that physical relocation is an S5 outcome and S4 only changes callers ('Changes callers, not file locations').
findings.location: Child decomposition S4 description vs. AC bullet for S4 and boundary note
scenarios: An implementer reading only the S4 child description will plan a physical file move in S4, conflicting with the AC's explicit constraint that relocation is deferred to S5, causing either duplicate work or a merge conflict when S5 lands.
findings.suggested_fix: Change S4 body to: 'route every core consumer through the `Backend` port — including ADF-sizing, lossy-status/parent, and duplicated maps/link-constants — by calling them via the adapter interface rather than importing them directly; physical files remain at the package root until S5.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `FakeBackend` (a minimal test-only backend implementing all five role Protocols) explicitly to the S2 child decomposition description so the AC deliverable is covered by described work.
evidence: AC: 'a contract suite passing against both `JiraBackend` and a test-only `FakeBackend` exist (S2)'
S2 description: 'define the `Backend` Protocols + `RemoteRef` + a backend-agnostic contract suite + a thin `JiraBackend` (zero behavior change). *No core rewiring.*' — no mention of FakeBackend
findings.finding: The AC for S2 requires a `FakeBackend` to exist ('a contract suite passing against both `JiraBackend` and a test-only `FakeBackend`'), but the S2 child decomposition description makes no mention of `FakeBackend` — it lists only 'Backend Protocols + RemoteRef + a backend-agnostic contract suite + a thin JiraBackend'. This is an orphaned AC deliverable with no corresponding described work.
findings.location: AC bullet for S1/S2 vs. S2 child decomposition description
scenarios: The S2 implementer, following only the child decomposition description, will not build FakeBackend. The AC will then fail at the end of S2, requiring rework or reassignment of FakeBackend to S3/S4 with unclear ownership.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to S2 description: '+ a minimal `FakeBackend` (test-only, implements all five role Protocols with no-op or stub behavior) used to validate the contract suite is backend-agnostic.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out statement for S5's hard-cut file relocation: specify whether reverting means a git revert of the S5 commit, or describe an alternative expand-contract option (e.g., keep shim imports for one release cycle), so the team knows the recovery path before merging.
evidence: 'physically relocate acli*/adf/outbound_fields/comment_limits into adapters/jira/… and migrate the ~29 test patch strings atomically (no shims)'
'Full suite … green at every landed change' — this is a pass/fail gate, not a rollback plan
No section describes what to do if S5's loader change fails to resolve modules at runtime after merging
findings.finding: S5's hard-cut relocation ('no re-export shims') is an explicit, justified choice, but the plan provides no explicit rollback/back-out path if S5 lands and breaks something in the loader or downstream consumers. The suite-green gate is a verification claim, not a reversibility mechanism; a brief statement of how to revert (e.g., git-revert the S5 commit, or the expand-contract option of keeping shims for one cycle) is absent.
findings.location: Child decomposition S5 / AC bullet 4 ('no re-export shims')
scenarios: S5 lands, the file-location loader has a bug resolving the new adapters/jira/ path in a deployment environment where package discovery differs; no shim exists to fall back on, rollback requires a full git revert of a large atomic commit.
A consumer outside the test suite (e.g., a script or doc-generation tool) imports the old root path and breaks silently post-S5 with no shim to catch it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' note to S5's description: 'If S5's loader change is found to be incorrect post-merge, revert via `git revert <S5-sha>`; because no shims exist, this is a clean revert with no dual-import state to clean up. Validate with `make test` before re-landing.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly include in S3's scope an update to the operator-facing configuration reference (config schema doc, README config section, or equivalent) documenting `reconciler.backend`, its default value (`"jira"`), accepted values, and validation behavior.
evidence: 'add `reconciler.backend` config (static-key validated) + an in-tree registry + `select_backend()` + `JiraBackend` registration'
ADR 0035 §(d) is described as the home for the interface spec, not operator/config documentation
No mention of updating a config schema file, README config section, or operator runbook for the new key
findings.finding: The new `config.reconciler.backend` configuration key (introduced in S3) is user-facing — operators must set it to select a backend — but the plan only mentions documenting the Backend Protocols and registry in ADR 0035 §(d). No explicit update to user-facing configuration documentation (config schema, README, or operator guide) is called out for this new key.
scenarios: An operator deploying the new version doesn't know the key exists, leaves it unset, and either gets a silent default ('jira') with no indication the key controls backend selection, or hits a validation error with no reference doc.
A future backend implementer (epic be74) looks for config documentation and finds only the ADR interface spec, not the config-key contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence rationale to the Design section (or S3) explaining why the in-tree decorator registry is chosen over `importlib.metadata` entry-points — specifically that all backends are in-tree by design (epic `be74` adds GitHub Issues as `adapters/github/`, not an installed plugin), making the simpler in-tree approach the right fit.
evidence: S3 description: 'The registry is a module-level dict populated by an idempotent register decorator (the in-tree decorator-registry style rebar uses elsewhere — no setuptools entry-points; a second backend is added in-tree under adapters/<x>/)'
ABSENCE: no sentence documents why importlib.metadata entry-points or pluggy-style hooks were considered and rejected
The plan's own constraint (epic be74 adds backends in-tree, never as installed plugins) would justify the choice, but that reasoning is not stated in the plan or S3
findings.finding: The plan introduces a custom in-tree decorator registry for backend selection (S3) and explicitly rejects setuptools/importlib.metadata entry-points, but provides no documented justification for why the established prior-art approach was ruled out. The only statement is 'no setuptools entry-points; a second backend is added in-tree under adapters/<x>/' with no rationale.
findings.location: Design section (in-tree decorator registry + select_backend); S3 ticket description 'no setuptools entry-points; a second backend is added in-tree under adapters/<x>/'
scenarios: A future reviewer implementing a third backend wonders whether they need to add a pip package or an in-tree adapters/<x>/ directory — the plan doesn't make the 'all backends are in-tree' invariant explicit, so the registry choice looks arbitrary
If a downstream fork ever wants to register an out-of-tree backend, they'll discover the registry choice forecloses that path without having been warned
findings.suggested_fix: In the Design section, under the 'An in-tree decorator registry + select_backend(config.reconciler.backend)' bullet, add: '(Entry-points / pluggy are not used because all backends are in-tree by policy — epic `be74` adds `adapters/github/`; no out-of-tree plugin install is expected.)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit 800-LOC module-size check (pytest tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py or equivalent) to the AC of both S2 (ce4d) and S4 (6673).
evidence: Parent AC: 'Every src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/*.py stays within the 800-LOC module-size cap (.github/module-size-limit.txt) throughout.'
S3 (94f9) AC: 'Every src/rebar/**/*.py stays within the 800-LOC cap — verified by pytest tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py, exit 0.'
S5 (dfb9) AC: 'Every src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/*.py stays within the 800-LOC module-size cap (.github/module-size-limit.txt) after the move — verified by the module-size gate.'
S2 (ce4d) AC: no module-size check present.
S4 (6673) AC: no module-size check present.
S4 expands adapters/jira/jira_fields.py with ADF-sizing, lossy-status, value-maps, link constants — the highest risk for a cap breach.
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: S2 (ce4d) and S4 (6673) — the two stories that introduce the most new code (Backend Protocols, JiraBackend wrapper, and the jira_fields.py logic expansion respectively) — have no explicit 800-LOC module-size cap check in their acceptance criteria, even though the parent requires the cap throughout ('at every landed change'). S3 and S5 both include an explicit module-size gate; S2 and S4 do not, leaving a gap during the two highest-risk landed states.
scenarios: S2 lands _backend.py with 8+ Protocols that exceeds 800 LOC; no gate catches it until S3.
S4 expands jira_fields.py with relocated logic that breaches 800 LOC; no gate catches it until S5.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S2 AC: 'Every src/rebar/**/*.py stays within the 800-LOC cap — verified by pytest tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py, exit 0.' Add same to S4 AC.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
